events.headline: Edison Lights the Night
events.body: Thomas Edison's incandescent bulb burns for forty hours in his Menlo Park lab, and within three years lower Manhattan is the first neighborhood on earth wired for electric light. The age of gaslight and candle is over.

events.headline: Alternating Current Wins the Current Wars
events.body: Westinghouse and Tesla's alternating-current system illuminates the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago, demonstrating that electricity can travel hundreds of miles without loss. Edison's DC empire concedes the grid to AC.

events.headline: Röntgen Sees Through Flesh
events.body: Wilhelm Röntgen accidentally discovers that cathode rays can expose photographic plates through solid objects, producing the first X-ray image of his wife's hand. Medicine will never look inside the body the same way again.

events.headline: The Wrights Leave the Ground
events.body: Orville Wright lifts off from a North Carolina sand dune for twelve seconds, covering 120 feet. His brother Wilbur will later fly 59 seconds over the same beach. Gravity has been negotiated.

events.headline: Einstein's Miraculous Year
events.body: A Swiss patent clerk named Albert Einstein publishes four papers that rewrite physics — including special relativity and the equivalence of mass and energy encoded in E=mc². Time and space will never again be absolute.

events.headline: A Trump Joins the MIT Physics Faculty
events.body: John G. Trump, electrical engineer and future MIT professor, begins his work on high-voltage electrostatic generators and radiation therapy. Donald's uncle will one day advise the government on the Tesla archives — a fact the family rarely advertises.

events.headline: Reality Gets Probabilistic
events.body: Heisenberg, Bohr, and Schrödinger formalize quantum mechanics at the Solvay Conference, establishing that at the atomic scale the universe runs on probability amplitudes, not clockwork. Einstein never accepts it; the atom doesn't care.

events.headline: Penicillin Saves Millions
events.body: American and British factories mass-produce Alexander Fleming's mold-derived antibiotic in time for the D-Day landings, slashing infection deaths. The age of infectious disease as a near-certain killer of the wounded begins its long retreat.

events.headline: Trinity: The First Atomic Flash
events.body: At 5:29 a.m. in the New Mexico desert, the world's first nuclear device detonates in a blinding fireball. J. Robert Oppenheimer recalls the Bhagavad Gita. Within weeks, two cities are ash. Physics has given civilization the power to end itself.

events.headline: Bell Labs Invents the Transistor
events.body: Three physicists at Bell Telephone Laboratories demonstrate a tiny semiconductor device that amplifies electrical signals without vacuum tubes. The transistor is the seed from which all modern computing grows.

events.headline: The Code of Life Is Decoded
events.body: Watson and Crick, building on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray crystallography, announce the double-helix structure of DNA — the molecular alphabet in which every organism's blueprint is written. Biology becomes an information science.

events.headline: Sputnik Beeps from Orbit
events.body: A Soviet basketball-sized satellite circles the Earth every 96 minutes, its radio signal audible to amateur operators worldwide. Washington panics. The Space Race has a starting gun, and America is already behind.

events.headline: One Giant Leap
events.body: Neil Armstrong steps onto the lunar surface and half a billion people watch on grainy television. The Moon — romanticized since human prehistory — now bears bootprints. Kennedy's impossible deadline has been met with five months to spare.

events.headline: Intel Puts a Computer on a Chip
events.body: Intel's 4004 microprocessor integrates 2,300 transistors on a sliver of silicon smaller than a thumbnail. The logic that once filled a room now fits in a palm, and the price will halve every two years for decades.

events.headline: The Computer Comes Home
events.body: Apple's Macintosh launches with a mouse and a graphical interface, and IBM-compatible PCs flood offices and dens. The terminal becomes a desktop, and every knowledge worker begins a long migration into the digital realm.

events.headline: Science Begins Reading the Book of Humanity
events.body: An international consortium launches the Human Genome Project, aiming to map every base pair in human DNA within fifteen years. The cost per genome is three billion dollars. The biological century is opening its first chapter.

events.headline: The Web Goes Public
events.body: Tim Berners-Lee releases the World Wide Web protocol into the public domain, and the first graphical browser lets anyone anywhere navigate a global library of linked pages. The information age stops being a metaphor.

events.headline: A Sheep Is Born Twice
events.body: Scottish scientists announce Dolly, the first mammal cloned from an adult somatic cell. The same DNA that made one sheep has made another, and the ethical and commercial implications for human medicine unspool slowly into the future.

events.headline: The Human Genome Is Complete
events.body: The Human Genome Project declares its draft finished: three billion base pairs catalogued. The cost was $2.7 billion and thirteen years. Within a decade, a consumer can sequence their own genome for a thousand dollars.

events.headline: A Supercomputer in Every Pocket
events.body: Steve Jobs introduces the iPhone — internet, phone, and iPod in a glass rectangle — and within a decade four billion people carry a more powerful computer than NASA had for the Apollo program. Reality is now always one tap away.

events.headline: Neural Nets Learn to See
events.body: Hinton's team's deep convolutional network obliterates the ImageNet benchmark by a margin that shocks computer vision researchers. Deep learning is no longer a fringe bet; GPU-trained neural networks begin their decade-long conquest of every perceptual task.

events.headline: Scientists Rewrite DNA with Molecular Scissors
events.body: Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier demonstrate CRISPR-Cas9, a cheap, precise tool for editing any gene in any organism. Inherited disease, designer crops, and the threshold of human germline modification all become near-term propositions.

events.headline: Rockets Learn to Land Themselves
events.body: SpaceX lands an orbital-class Falcon 9 booster upright on a drone ship in the Atlantic, slashing the cost of reaching orbit by an order of magnitude. A private company has done what NASA never did, and the economics of spaceflight will never recover from the disruption.

events.headline: Machines Learn to Talk Back
events.body: Large language models trained on most of the written internet converse, code, and reason at human graduate-school level, and within months hundreds of millions use them daily. The line between human-authored and machine-authored text begins its permanent blur.

events.headline: The Last Benchmark Falls
events.body: An AI system passes every remaining professional certification and scientific reasoning benchmark simultaneously, surpassing expert human performance across all cognitive domains. Governments convene emergency sessions; the labs are already building the next version.

events.headline: Fusion Power Goes Net Positive — and Stays There
events.body: A commercial inertial-confinement reactor in southern France produces sustained net energy for the first time and feeds power into the grid for 72 continuous hours. The promise made in 1950 is finally kept; the age of fossil scarcity formally ends.

events.headline: Aging Is Reclassified as a Treatable Disease
events.body: A combination of senolytics, epigenetic reprogramming, and targeted gene therapy extends healthy human lifespan beyond 130 years in clinical trials. The FDA reclassifies biological aging as a disease; the billionaire class books the first slots.

events.headline: The First Crewed Ship Departs for Mars
events.body: A multinational crew of twelve departs Earth aboard a fusion-powered vessel, carrying the genome archive, a library of humanity's knowledge, and orders to build a permanent settlement on the Martian surface. The voyage takes eleven weeks.

events.headline: Ares Base Achieves Self-Sufficiency
events.body: The Martian settlement crosses 1,000 permanent residents and completes its first closed-loop life-support year — food grown, air recycled, water reclaimed entirely on-world. For the first time, a human community does not depend on Earth to survive.

events.headline: Quantum Gravity Is Solved
events.body: A team working across three continents and two planets publishes a unified field theory reconciling general relativity with quantum mechanics, predicting stable traversable topologies at energies achievable within two generations. Physicists call it the most important paper since 1905.

events.headline: The Alcubierre Drive Leaves the Whiteboard
events.body: A prototype warp-metric generator at the Martian research station bends local spacetime by a measurable 0.0003%, confirming the underlying physics of faster-than-light travel. No ship moves yet, but the road to the stars is now a matter of engineering, not theory.

events.headline: First Human Ship Clears the Heliopause
events.body: A crewed vessel departs from the Martian L2 point using a scaled warp-metric drive, reaching a velocity of 0.08c and exiting the solar system in eleven days — the first humans to leave our sun's influence. The probe transmits a single message: 'still here.'

events.headline: The Signal Is Not Natural
events.body: Deep-space array telescopes lock onto a mathematically structured repeating pulse from the direction of Tau Ceti — prime-number sequences embedded in a carrier wave no known astrophysical process can produce. Scientists spend two years ruling out every alternative before making the announcement.

events.headline: First Contact Confirmed
events.body: A crewed interstellar vessel following the Tau Ceti signal makes visual and electromagnetic contact with an artificial structure of non-human origin. The United Earth government receives the message eighteen hours later. The species is no longer alone.
